text: A total of 16 single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) across 14 genes have been associated with marathon running performance in elite athletes.
Predicting future marathon success based on genomic data alone is premature due to the lack of replicated genotype-phenotype association studies.
Endurance-related traits relevant to marathon performance have a heritable genetic basis, though the specific genetic architecture involves multiple genes with small individual effects.
Across 14 eligible studies, 160 different polymorphisms in 27 genes were examined in relation to marathon running performance, indicating high genetic complexity of the endurance phenotype.

Publication bias and lack of replication across studies limit the reliability of current gene-marathon performance associations, so coaches should not use genetic testing to select or exclude athletes.
Epigenetic factors (gene expression changes from training, environment, and lifestyle) may be as important as the underlying genetic sequence in determining marathon performance.
